does: This survey provides a comprehensive overview of the AI alignment field by identifying four core principles (Robustness, Interpretability, Controllability, and Ethicality) that guide alignment research and organizing current work into two complementary categories. Forward alignment encompasses techniques for training aligned AI systems, including methods for learning from human feedback and adapting to distribution shifts. Backward alignment covers approaches to verify and govern alignment, including assurance techniques for detecting misalignment and governance practices for risk mitigation. The paper synthesizes existing methodologies, practice, and conceptual frameworks across these domains to establish a structured understanding of how the field approaches the fundamental problem of ensuring AI systems behave according to human intentions and values.
connects: This work builds on decades of research in AI safety, ethics, and control that recognized misalignment as a central risk as AI systems became more capable. It synthesizes and organizes emerging subfields including interpretability research (work on understanding neural networks), reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and preference learning approaches, robustness and adversarial training methods, and formal verification techniques. The survey connects to earlier value alignment proposals, work on corrigibility and impact measures, and governance frameworks from technology policy. It integrates perspectives from machine learning safety, philosophy, and policy communities that have independently tackled aspects of alignment, creating a unifying framework that acknowledges both the technical training challenges and the verification-and-governance components required for safe AI deployment.
enables: By providing a structured landscape of alignment research, this survey enables researchers to identify gaps between current capabilities and the alignment guarantees needed for increasingly autonomous systems. It supports the development of integrated approaches combining multiple alignment techniques rather than isolated solutions. The framework points toward future work on scalable oversight methods that can handle complex AI behaviors, integration of alignment objectives during training rather than as post-hoc corrections, and governance frameworks that combine technical assurance with institutional safeguards. The taxonomy facilitates cross-disciplinary collaboration between ML researchers, interpretability specialists, policy experts, and ethicists. It also enables practitioners deploying AI systems to assess alignment maturity and select appropriate combinations of forward and backward alignment techniques for their specific risk contexts.
